name: David Frangioni
jobTitle: CEO & Publisher, Modern Drummer Magazine / Founder, Frangioni Media / Founder & CEO, All Access IDA
description:
David Frangioni did not choose music. Music chose him at two years old — through a drum kit, and through something else: eye cancer. He survived the cancer as a toddler and found salvation, structure, and identity in rhythm. By the time he was 12 years old, he was playing professional gigs in the clubs of Boston, navigating adult spaces with a drummer's composure and a teenager's wonder. By 16, he had started a music technology consulting business — not a hobby project, but a functioning commercial operation — built around MIDI programming, one of the earliest and most technical frontiers of digital music production.

That business caught the attention of Tom Hamilton, the bass player of Aerosmith, who introduced David to the band in 1989. What followed was 13 years as Aerosmith's in-house recording engineer and technologist — a role that encompassed running the console, managing computers and recorders, engineering records, solving production problems during the most high-pressure moments of the creative process, and eventually co-creating the theme song for the Spider-Man animated television series with Joe Perry. Seven albums. Sales of over 40 million records. Gold and platinum certifications. And a front-row seat to one of rock music's most remarkable careers during one of its most technically transformative periods.

During that same period, David was also building the first all-Pro Tools digital recording studio — a milestone in music production history. It was in that studio that Ricky Martin's 'Livin' La Vida Loca' was recorded, one of the most commercially successful pop recordings of the era, made possible by a technical infrastructure David helped design and implement.

From his base in Miami, Florida, David founded Audio One Sound & Video Inc. in the mid-1990s and built it from a cold start into the premier audio-visual systems integration company in the United States and internationally. He led Audio One to more CEDIA awards in a given time period than any other AV integrator in the world. Alongside that business, he built Frangioni Media — a distinguished AV and home technology integration firm serving the southeastern coast of Florida — and cultivated working relationships with artists including the Rolling Stones, Ozzy Osbourne, Ringo Starr, Elton John, Sting, Bryan Adams, Journey, Phil Collins, Shakira, Cher, and Chick Corea.

Today David Frangioni is the CEO and Publisher of Modern Drummer Magazine, the world's number one drum brand for more than 45 years, and the founder and CEO of All Access IDA, an artist development firm. He has published three books through Insight Editions, including 'Crash: The World's Greatest Drum Kits' and two volumes of 'Clint Eastwood: Icon.' He is a board member of Musicians On Call (President's Council), Musical Director for Irie Foundation, and leads the Frangioni Foundation. He appeared on the Center Stage segment of Mornings in the Lab to discuss ambition, the quiet discipline of building something real, and why the dream doesn't require waiting for the right moment — only clarity, patience, and showing up.
knowsAbout: Music technology and recording engineering at elite levels
Pro Tools and digital audio workstation (DAW) production history
Aerosmith and classic rock studio production
Drum performance, MIDI programming, and music production
Audio-visual systems integration (CEDIA, luxury home theater)
Artist development and creative career strategy
Modern Drummer Magazine and the global drumming community
Immersive audio and Dolby Atmos conversion for music and film
Eye cancer survival and music as an anchor through adversity
Entrepreneurship: from MIDI consultant at 16 to multi-company founder
Philanthropy: Musicians On Call, Irie Foundation, Frangioni Foundation

episode.title: He Built The First All-Digital Studio & Changed Music Forever

keyInsights.quote: Age two was actually the best thing that ever happened to your career, fellas.
keyInsights.context: David's darkly funny opening line — referring to surviving eye cancer at two years old — is his way of reframing the cancer not as a tragedy but as the origin of his relationship with music. The drum kit he found in recovery became his anchor, and that anchor became a career. His capacity to reframe hardship as foundation is a recurring pattern in his story.
keyInsights.topic: Surviving cancer as the origin of a musical career

keyInsights.quote: By 12, he was playing professional gigs in Boston clubs.
keyInsights.context: The specificity of this fact matters: 12 years old in adult Boston clubs, being paid for music. This is not 'he showed promise'; this is professional-level performance at an age when most children are still in middle school. It establishes the timeline of his commitment and the pace at which he was developing.
keyInsights.topic: Professional performance at 12: the early trajectory

keyInsights.quote: At 16, David started a music technology business — not a hobby, a real operation.
keyInsights.context: The host's insistence on distinguishing a 'real operation' from a hobby is significant. David did not dabble — he built. At 16, in the early era of MIDI technology, he identified a technical frontier and commercialized his expertise before most adults in the industry understood what MIDI was.
keyInsights.topic: Starting a real technology business at 16

keyInsights.quote: He helped build the first all-Pro Tools studio used to record Ricky Martin's Livin' La Vida Loca.
keyInsights.context: This is a landmark of music production history. Pro Tools as an all-digital recording environment was, in the late 1990s, a genuinely radical departure from the industry standard. David was not an early adopter — he was one of the architects of the shift, and the recording it produced became one of the best-selling singles of the era.
keyInsights.topic: The first all-digital studio and music production history

keyInsights.quote: The dream isn't impossible. It just requires clarity, patience, and showing up long after motivation fades.
keyInsights.context: David's distillation of what sustained him through decades of work in a brutal, high-turnover industry. This is not motivational language — it is the operational description of how he actually built his career, through the unglamorous consistency of showing up at the technical frontier when others moved on.
keyInsights.topic: Clarity, patience, and showing up after motivation fades

keyInsights.quote: Working with Aerosmith since 1989 — thirteen years of engineering, albums, tours, and the theme song for Spider-Man with Joe Perry.
keyInsights.context: Thirteen years is an extraordinary tenure in a relationship-driven industry where most technical relationships are transactional and brief. David's longevity with Aerosmith reflects not just competence but trust — the kind built by being present, reliable, and genuinely contributing to the band's creative and commercial success over multiple albums and tours.
keyInsights.topic: 13 years with Aerosmith: technical trust at the highest level

keyInsights.quote: He led Audio One to more CEDIA awards in a given time period than any other audio-visual system integrator in the world.
keyInsights.context: CEDIA (Custom Electronic Design & Installation Association) awards are the industry benchmark for excellence in AV integration. David's achievement of more awards per period than any global competitor reflects the same standard-setting approach he applied to music technology — identify the frontier, go deep, and become the best at it.
keyInsights.topic: Audio One: the world's most awarded AV integrator

keyInsights.quote: He found salvation behind a drum kit.
keyInsights.context: The framing of drums as salvation — not hobby, not talent, but salvation — signals that music was never merely a career for David. It was, from the beginning, existential. That intensity of relationship with the craft is what explains both his early professional trajectory and the depth of his engagement with it across every medium he has inhabited.
keyInsights.topic: Music as salvation: the existential relationship with the craft

faqs.question: Who is David Frangioni and what is he known for in music technology?
faqs.answer: David Frangioni is an award-winning music technologist, audio engineer, producer, drummer, author, and media executive based in Miami, Florida. He is best known as Aerosmith's in-house recording engineer and technologist from 1989 to 2002 — working on seven albums with total sales of more than 40 million records, multiple tours, and the Spider-Man animated theme song. He helped build the first all-Pro Tools digital recording studio, used to record Ricky Martin's 'Livin' La Vida Loca.' He is the founder of Audio One Sound & Video Inc. (which became the world's most CEDIA-awarded AV integrator), Frangioni Media, and All Access IDA. He is also the CEO and Publisher of Modern Drummer Magazine. He has worked with artists including the Rolling Stones, Ozzy Osbourne, Elton John, Sting, Ringo Starr, Bryan Adams, and Shakira.

faqs.question: What is David Frangioni's connection to Aerosmith?
faqs.answer: David Frangioni served as Aerosmith's in-house recording engineer and technologist from 1989 to approximately 2002 — a 13-year relationship he has continued to maintain in various capacities. He was introduced to the band by bass player Tom Hamilton after establishing himself through his MIDI technology consulting business. In his role, David ran the console, managed studio computers and recording systems, engineered records, and solved production problems during the songwriting and recording process. He worked on seven albums with Aerosmith, contributing to the band's most commercially successful era, and helped produce the theme song for the Spider-Man animated television series with guitarist Joe Perry — Joe's first all-digital recording.

faqs.question: What is Modern Drummer Magazine and what is David Frangioni's role?
faqs.answer: Modern Drummer Magazine is the world's number one drum publication, with a history of more than 45 years covering drumming, percussion, equipment, education, and the business of drumming. David Frangioni serves as its CEO and Publisher, overseeing the brand across its print, digital, and community platforms. The magazine has featured generations of the world's most celebrated drummers and serves as the definitive authority in the global drumming community. David's own lifelong relationship with drums — beginning at age two and spanning professional performance by age 12 — makes him a uniquely credible steward of the publication.

faqs.question: What is Frangioni Media and what services does it offer?
faqs.answer: Frangioni Media is an audio-video and home technology integration company based in Miami and serving the southeastern coast of Florida, specializing in luxury home theater design, professional studio installation, high-end AV systems, and whole-home automation. The company has worked on world-class projects for artists including Elton John, Cher, the Rolling Stones, Aerosmith, and many others. Frangioni Media is also an immersive audio specialist, converting stereo and surround sound projects to immersive audio formats for music and film professionals. It represents the continuation of the AV integration expertise David built through Audio One Sound & Video Inc. in the 1990s.

faqs.question: What books has David Frangioni written?
faqs.answer: David Frangioni has published three books through Insight Editions: 'Crash: The World's Greatest Drum Kits' (a comprehensive visual and editorial celebration of legendary drum kits from Neil Peart, Vinnie Appice, Alex Van Halen, and many others), and two volumes of 'Clint Eastwood: Icon' (covering the filmography and cultural legacy of the legendary director and actor). His books reflect the breadth of his creative interests — spanning music, celebrity culture, and visual storytelling — and have established him as an author with access and relationships at the highest levels of entertainment.

faqs.question: What is All Access IDA and how does David Frangioni work with artists?
faqs.answer: All Access IDA (International Digital Artist) is the artist development firm founded and led by David Frangioni. Through All Access IDA, David works with artists and creators who want greater control over their craft, their technology, and their career trajectory. The firm provides strategic guidance on studio design, recording technology, production workflows, and the business decisions that determine whether an artist can build a sustainable career over the long term. David's perspective is shaped by 30+ years of direct work at the intersection of music, technology, and commerce — making him one of the few people in the industry who can advise authentically on both the creative and technical dimensions of what it takes to build something that lasts.

conversationTopics.label: The open: working hard without knowing if it's real
conversationTopics.summary: The episode opens with the universal question David's life answers: can you keep building when you can't see the endpoint? The setup positions him as someone who has lived inside that doubt and kept moving.

conversationTopics.label: Eye cancer at two and the drum kit
conversationTopics.summary: David discusses surviving eye cancer as a toddler and finding drums in the aftermath — music as salvation, rhythm as structure. He opens with a self-aware joke about 'age two being the best thing to happen to his career.'

conversationTopics.label: Playing professional gigs at 12 in Boston
conversationTopics.summary: David recounts his early performance career — playing adult venues in Boston at 12 years old, how he carried himself in those spaces, and what performing at that level taught him about professionalism and discipline.

conversationTopics.label: Starting a technology business at 16
conversationTopics.summary: The MIDI consulting business David built at 16 — what MIDI was, why it was a frontier, and how his technical capability became a commercial asset long before he understood what a career in music technology would look like.

conversationTopics.label: Meeting Aerosmith and becoming their in-house engineer
conversationTopics.summary: David describes the introduction through Tom Hamilton, the beginning of a 13-year working relationship, what the role actually entailed day-to-day, and what it was like to be inside the creative and commercial machinery of one of rock's biggest bands.

conversationTopics.label: The first all-digital studio and Livin' La Vida Loca
conversationTopics.summary: David discusses the milestone of building the first all-Pro Tools studio and its role in recording Ricky Martin's breakthrough hit — what the technical environment looked like, what was at stake, and why it mattered for the industry.

conversationTopics.label: Audio One and the AV integration business
conversationTopics.summary: David describes founding Audio One in the mid-1990s, scaling it to the most CEDIA-awarded AV integration company in the world, and the business of bringing world-class sound and technology to artists and high-end residential clients.

conversationTopics.label: Modern Drummer Magazine and the publishing role
conversationTopics.summary: David discusses becoming CEO and Publisher of Modern Drummer — the world's number one drum brand — and what it means to him personally as a drummer to be the steward of the publication's legacy.

conversationTopics.label: All Access IDA and artist development today
conversationTopics.summary: David explains what he does through All Access IDA — working with artists who want control over their craft and career — and how the philosophy of his work has evolved from technical problem-solving to strategic life-design.

conversationTopics.label: Clarity, patience, and showing up after motivation fades
conversationTopics.summary: David reflects on what has sustained his career across multiple decades, industries, and ventures — and delivers the core message of his Center Stage appearance: the dream requires not talent, but the discipline to keep showing up.

conversationTopics.label: Closing and how to connect
conversationTopics.summary: Sophia closes the segment with David, directing viewers to davidfrangioni.com, frangionimedia.com, and moderndrummer.com.

pullQuotes: The dream isn't impossible. It just requires clarity, patience, and showing up long after motivation fades.
By 12, he was playing professional gigs in Boston clubs.
He helped build the first all-Pro Tools studio used to record Ricky Martin's Livin' La Vida Loca.
guestEngine.fullTranscript: If you've ever worked your ass off and wondered if the dream is slipping further away, you're not alone. You do the work. You stay disciplined. And still, there's that quiet question, am I actually building towards something real? Today, we're meeting David, someone who's lived inside that question and kept moving. As a child, David survived eye cancer. That experience reshaped his world early, and music became his anchor. By 12, he was playing professional gigs in Boston clubs. While most kids were still discovering their interests, at 16, David started a music technology business, not a hobby, a real operation. That path led him to become Aerosmith's in-house recording engineer and technologist for over 13 years. He helped design studios, engineer records, and solve problems when the pressure was high and the stakes were real. He also helped build the first all-Pro Tools studio used to record Ricky Martin's Livin' La Vida Loca. Today, David works with artists and creators who want control over their craft, their tools, and the life they're building. He believes the dream isn't impossible. It just requires clarity, patience, and showing up long after motivation fades. Today, we're talking ambition, passion, technology, and the grit it takes to build something that actually lasts. David, we're excited to have you on Mornings in the Lab.
